content.text: Cost telemetry answers the question: how much does each LLM request cost, and how does that aggregate to a monthly bill? Without per-request cost attribution, you cannot identify expensive workloads, enforce budget guardrails, or accurately price your API product. Cost in LLM serving is primarily GPU compute — with egress, storage, and operational overhead adding 10–30% on top.

attrs.alt: LLM cost attribution model: GPU compute + storage + egress + overhead → cost per output token, broken down by request type

content.text: GPU compute cost is billed by instance-hour, regardless of whether the GPU is running inference or sitting idle. For self-hosted deployments:
A p4de.24xlarge (8×A100 80GB) on AWS on-demand costs ~$32/hr. At 600 output tokens/s sustained throughput, that is:

attrs.latex: \text{Cost per output token} = \frac{\$32/\text{hr}}{600 \text{ tok/s} \times 3600 \text{ s/hr}} = \$0.0000148 = \$14.8/\text{M tokens}

content.text: Spot instances cut this 60–70% (to ~$5–6/hr) at the cost of potential preemption. Reserved instances (1–3 year) cut it 30–40%. GPU utilisation is the efficiency multiplier: an idle GPU running at 10% utilisation costs 10× more per token than a busy one at 90%.
Prefill and decode have different compute profiles and thus different costs per token:
10× more expensive per token than prefill

Output tokens cost roughly 10× more than input tokens because each decode step loads the full 140 GB model from HBM regardless of batch size (until batch is large enough to be compute-bound). This is why API providers like OpenAI charge 3–5× more for output tokens than input tokens.
S3/GCS charges ~$0.023/GB/month. 140 GB for 70B BF16 ≈ $3.2/month per region — negligible compared to GPU cost.
KV cache lives in GPU HBM, not persistent storage, so no direct storage charge. But KV cache capacity determines maximum batch size, which determines GPU count.

Streaming output tokens cross the network. AWS charges $0.09/GB for outbound data. A 1000-token response at 4 bytes/token = 4 KB. At 10M requests/day: 40 GB/day = $3.60/day = ~$108/month. Large for high-traffic services.
CloudWatch Logs, Prometheus remote write, trace storage. Typically $50–200/month for a medium-sized service — track separately.

# Hourly cost rate in dollars
sum(rate(llm_request_cost_dollars_total[1h])) * 3600

# Cost per million output tokens (efficiency metric)
sum(rate(llm_request_cost_dollars_total[1h]))
  /
sum(rate(llm_output_tokens_total[1h]))
* 1_000_000

# Cost by tenant (for multi-tenant billing)
sum(rate(llm_request_cost_dollars_total[1h])) by (tenant_id) * 3600

# GPU efficiency: how much of GPU cost is generating tokens vs idle
sum(rate(llm_output_tokens_total[5m])) * 0.000010   # output token cost rate
  /
(gpu_instance_count * gpu_cost_per_second)           # total GPU cost rate

# Key Grafana panels for cost monitoring
panels:
  - title: "Hourly Spend ($)"
    type: stat
    targets:
      - expr: sum(rate(llm_request_cost_dollars_total[1h])) * 3600
    fieldConfig:
      unit: currencyUSD
      decimals: 2

  - title: "Cost per Million Output Tokens"
    type: timeseries
    targets:
      - expr: |
          sum(rate(llm_request_cost_dollars_total[30m]))
            / sum(rate(llm_output_tokens_total[30m]))
            * 1e6
    fieldConfig:
      unit: currencyUSD

  - title: "Cost by Tenant (Top 10)"
    type: bargauge
    targets:
      - expr: |
          topk(10,
            sum(rate(llm_request_cost_dollars_total[1h])) by (tenant_id) * 3600
          )

  - title: "Daily Spend vs Budget"
    type: gauge
    targets:
      - expr: |
          sum(increase(llm_request_cost_dollars_total[24h]))
    fieldConfig:
      min: 0
      max: 500   # daily budget $500
      thresholds:
        - value: 400
          color: yellow
        - value: 480
          color: red

groups:
- name: llm_cost
  rules:
  - alert: DailySpendApproachingBudget
    expr: |
      sum(increase(llm_request_cost_dollars_total[24h])) > 450
    labels:
      severity: warning
    annotations:
      summary: "Daily spend ${{ $value | printf "%.2f" }} approaching $500 budget"

  - alert: CostPerTokenSpike
    expr: |
      (
        sum(rate(llm_request_cost_dollars_total[15m]))
          / sum(rate(llm_output_tokens_total[15m]))
          * 1e6
      ) > 25
    for: 5m
    labels:
      severity: warning
    annotations:
      summary: "Cost/M tokens > $25 — GPU utilisation may have dropped"
      description: "Check output throughput. Low throughput means idle GPU cost spreading over fewer tokens."
The single most impactful variable. Cost per token is inversely proportional to utilisation:

attrs.latex: \text{Cost per token} = \frac{\text{GPU cost/s}}{\text{tok/s at current utilisation}}

content.text: At 10% GPU utilisation: 180 tok/s on an H100×8 → $0.054/s ÷ 180 tok/s = $300/M tokens. At 80% utilisation: 1440 tok/s → $37/M tokens. Target 70–80% sustained GPU utilisation by right-sizing the fleet and using autoscaling to match capacity to demand.

When multiple requests share a long system prompt (common in RAG and chat products), vLLM's prefix cache serves the prefill from cache rather than recomputing it. This reduces prefill compute to near-zero for the shared prefix:

# Enable prefix caching in vLLM (default: off)
python -m vllm.entrypoints.openai.api_server \
  --model meta-llama/Llama-3-70b-instruct \
  --enable-prefix-caching \
  --max-num-seqs 64

# Measure cache hit rate
curl http://localhost:8000/metrics | grep prefix_cache
# vllm:gpu_prefix_cache_hit_rate 0.73   ← 73% of prefill tokens served from cache
At 73% prefix cache hit rate, effective prefill cost drops 73%. For a workload with 2000-token system prompts and 100-token outputs, this cuts total per-request cost by ~40%.
Quantisation reduces model size and memory bandwidth requirements, allowing more tokens per second from the same hardware:

# Serve FP8 quantised model with vLLM
python -m vllm.entrypoints.openai.api_server \
  --model neuralmagic/Meta-Llama-3-70B-Instruct-FP8 \
  --dtype float8

# Serve AWQ INT4 quantised model
python -m vllm.entrypoints.openai.api_server \
  --model casperhansen/llama-3-70b-instruct-awq \
  --quantization awq

# Kubernetes: use spot instances for vLLM pods with on-demand fallback
# Node pool: spot GPU nodes (60-70% cheaper)
# Tolerations + node selector target spot nodes
# PodDisruptionBudget ensures at least 1 on-demand pod stays up

# Example: 3 spot + 1 on-demand for a 4-pod deployment
# At 60% spot discount: cost = (3 × $9.6 + 1 × $32) / 4 = $15.2/hr avg
# vs all on-demand: 4 × $32 = $128/hr
# Savings: 88% — BUT spot can be preempted at any time

# Safe pattern: use spot for batch/offline inference,
# on-demand for interactive latency-sensitive serving
For SaaS products, you need to track cost per tenant to price accurately and identify high-consumption customers. The key is propagating tenant identity from the request through to cost counters:

from fastapi import Request
import jwt

async def extract_tenant(request: Request) -> str:
    auth = request.headers.get("Authorization", "")
    if auth.startswith("Bearer "):
        try:
            payload = jwt.decode(auth[7:], options={"verify_signature": False})
            return payload.get("tenant_id", "unknown")
        except Exception:
            pass
    return "unknown"

# In your completions handler:
async def completions(request: Request, body: dict):
    tenant = await extract_tenant(request)
    # ... call vLLM ...
    record_request_cost(
        model=body["model"],
        tenant_id=tenant,
        input_tokens=response["usage"]["prompt_tokens"],
        output_tokens=response["usage"]["completion_tokens"],
    )

Report monthly cost per tenant to: (a) bill customers accurately, (b) identify the top 10% of tenants consuming 80%+ of capacity (Pareto effect is common), and (c) set per-tenant rate limits proportional to their plan.
Tracking cost by request count, not tokens:
Request count is misleading when request sizes vary 100× (50-token chat vs 5000-token RAG). Always track input and output token counts separately.
Reserving 8 GPUs 24/7 costs the same whether they serve 1 req/hr or 1000 req/hr. Off-hours cost should trigger scale-to-zero or smaller instance size.
Output tokens are 5–10× more expensive to generate than input tokens (per decode step vs per prefill step). Pricing them equally undercharges for long generations.
No cost attribution to model version:
When you switch from a 70B to an 8B model for certain request types, cost per token drops dramatically. Tag cost by model name to see the impact.
Using spot instances for long batch jobs without checkpointing means a preemption loses hours of work. Always checkpoint batch jobs to S3 every N steps.

LLM cost telemetry requires per-request token counting (input and output separately), a cost model ($/token by model), and Prometheus counters that accumulate cost by model and tenant. Output tokens are ~10× more expensive than input tokens — reflect this in pricing and dashboards. The main optimisation levers are GPU utilisation (target 70–80%), prefix caching (40–70% prefill reduction for shared system prompts), quantisation (FP8/INT4 cut cost 30–50%), and spot instances for batch workloads.
